text:
I keep a sticky note on my monitor that says "You are not the single thread."

It’s from a debugging session at 2am three weeks ago. I was manually fixing an agent prompt instead of building a system that catches the drift automatically.

Old habit. 44 years of "I’ll just do it myself" is hard to unwire.

The whole point of building an agent army is to stop being the bottleneck. Every time I catch myself hand-fixing something, I stop and ask: can this be a tool instead?

Usually the answer is yes. The sticky note stays.

text: Blog: Ghost Posts and the Watchdog That Cried Wolf — source slug: ghost-posts-and-the-watchdog-that-cried-wolf. Atomize into tweets: ghost posts concept, watchdog spam story, bash date math bug, the monitor-the-monitor principle, elegant solutions failing in production.

Most cannabis operators I talk to have the same problem: their seed-to-sale system is a compliance checkbox, not an operational tool.

Metrc. BioTrack. Leaf Data. Pick your state, pick your system. They all do the same thing: track plant movement for the regulator.

None of them were built for the operator.

So what happens? Your team builds spreadsheets around the tracking system. Shadow inventory. Manual reconciliation. A compliance manager who spends 60% of their time making sure the spreadsheet matches the state system.

I've seen this pattern before. At Riverbed, every enterprise had shadow IT. Tools the company didn't sanction because the official tools didn't work for the people using them. The fix was never 'stop using the shadow tools.' The fix was making the official tools actually useful.

Same thing in cannabis. The fix isn't yelling at your team for using spreadsheets. It's building an intelligence layer on top of the tracking system that actually serves operations.

AI agents sitting between your team and the state system. Flagging discrepancies before they become violations. Auto-reconciling inventory. Surfacing the data your growers actually need.

The tracking system stays. The spreadsheets disappear. Your compliance manager does actual compliance work.

15 years of translating complex systems into operational leverage. Cannabis is just the latest domain.
notes: Cannabis ops: seed-to-sale shadow IT pattern. Connects Riverbed enterprise experience to cannabis. Fills LinkedIn + cannabis_ops gap.

text:
Saturday mornings used to be meetings. Board prep. Slide decks about pipeline coverage.

Now they're coffee and terminal windows.

My wife thinks I'm working. I'm not. I'm watching my agents run. Checking the overnight logs. Seeing what decisions they made while I slept.

It's the same feeling I had at 10 years old with that Osborne Executive. Not productive. Just curious.

44 years in and the thing that keeps me building isn't the outcome. It's the process. The quiet hour before anyone needs anything, when you can just look at what you built and think about what's next.

The agents don't care that it's Saturday. They ran their cycles. Filed their reports. Flagged two things for my attention. I'll get to them after this coffee.

text:
Unpopular opinion: the best AI implementations in cannabis won't come from tech companies.

They'll come from operators who got tired of waiting.

Every enterprise AI vendor wants to sell you a platform. 6-month implementation. Custom integrations. Annual contracts. By the time you're live, the regulations changed twice.

The operators building their own agent infrastructure — even ugly, duct-tape versions — are learning something the vendors can't teach: how YOUR operation actually works.

I built my compliance automation because no vendor understood our workflow. Not because they're bad. Because cannabis operations are genuinely weird. Every state different. Every facility different. The edge cases ARE the cases.

The operator advantage isn't technical sophistication. It's domain knowledge that no outside team can replicate.

Build ugly. Ship fast. Own the decisions. That's how you win in the hardest regulatory environment in America.
notes: Cannabis engagement bait: operator advantage over vendors. Contrarian angle. Fills LinkedIn + engagement_bait gap.

text:
Real question for founders building with AI agents:

How do you decide what stays manual?

5 weeks running a 24-agent system and my biggest lesson isn't what to automate. It's what NOT to.

My agents draft content, run compliance checks, monitor brand safety, handle scheduling. They're great at repeatable decisions with clear criteria.

But pricing strategy? Client relationships? Hiring calls? Those stay with me. Not because agents can't do them — because the cost of being wrong is too high and the feedback loop is too slow.

The automation boundary isn't about capability. It's about error tolerance.

What's your line? What stays manual no matter how good the tools get?
notes: Founder engagement bait for Threads. Error-tolerance framework. Drives replies from builders. Fills founder + engagement_bait + Threads gaps.

text:
Cannabis operators: your tracking system knows where every plant is.

Does it know which ones are profitable?

Most seed-to-sale platforms track compliance. Zero of them track unit economics at the plant level. You know your total COGS. You have no idea which strains, which rooms, which harvest cycles actually make money.

I've watched operators cut their best-performing strain because the aggregate numbers looked bad. The strain was fine. The room it shared with a problem crop was dragging the average.

This is the same mistake I saw in enterprise IT for 15 years. Aggregate dashboards hide the signal. You need per-unit intelligence.

AI agents can do this math in real time. Match every input cost to every plant. Surface which grows are printing money and which are burning it. Before you make the cut decision, not after.

Who else is trying to solve plant-level economics? Curious what approaches are working.
notes: Cannabis engagement bait for Threads. Plant-level economics angle. Enterprise IT pattern match. Fills cannabis + engagement_bait + Threads gaps.

text:
The hardest part of building an agent army isn't the agents. It's trusting them.

Week 1 I reviewed every output. Week 2 I spot-checked half. Week 3 I built monitoring dashboards. Week 4 I started sleeping through overnight cycles.

Week 5 I realized the dashboards were my security blanket. The agents had been fine the whole time. I was the bottleneck pretending to be quality control.

Same pattern I saw at Riverbed when we'd deploy automation for Fortune 500 customers. The technology was ready months before the humans were. Every org had someone who couldn't stop checking.

Founder lesson: your trust timeline IS your scaling timeline. You can't grow past the things you won't let go of.

The agent army doesn't need you watching. It needs you building the next thing.
notes: Founder value teaching for X. Trust timeline framework. Riverbed parallel. Fills founder + value_teaching gap.

text:
The cannabis compliance problem isn't documentation. It's timing.

Every operator I talk to has the records. Manifests filed. METRC entries logged. Waste documented. The paper trail exists.

What kills them is the gap between when something happens and when it gets recorded.

Harvest at 6am. Paperwork at 4pm. 10-hour window where your physical inventory doesn't match your digital inventory. That's not negligence. That's a crew working fast in a facility with spotty WiFi and a state system that crashes every Thursday.

At Riverbed we called this 'eventual consistency' — a distributed systems concept where different nodes have different versions of the truth, and the system reconciles over time.

Cannabis facilities are distributed systems. Multiple rooms. Multiple workers. Multiple data entry points. The state expects real-time consistency from an operation that is architecturally eventually consistent.

AI agents close that gap. Mobile capture at point of action. Automatic METRC sync. Discrepancy alerts before they become violations.

The compliance problem was never about discipline. It was about architecture.
notes: Cannabis value teaching for LinkedIn. Eventual consistency framework from distributed systems. Riverbed bridge. Fills cannabis + LinkedIn gap.
